sections.label: Section 190
sections.original: σκαιοὺς δὲ λέγων κοὐδέν τι σοφοὺς
sections.translation: Wert thou to call the men of old time rude uncultured boors thou wouldst not err , seeing that they devised their hymns for festive occasions , for banquets , and to grace the board , a pleasure to catch the ear , shed o ’ er our life ,

sections.label: Section 195
sections.original: στυγίους δὲ βροτῶν οὐδεὶς λύπας
sections.translation: but no man hath found a way to allay hated grief by music and the minstrel ’ s varied strain , whence arise slaughters and fell strokes of fate to o ’ erthrow the homes of men . And yet this were surely a gain ,

sections.label: Section 200
sections.original: μολπαῖσι βροτούς · ἵνα δ ’ εὔδειπνοι
sections.translation: to heal men ’ s wounds by music ’ s spell , but why tune they their idle song where rich banquets are spread ? for of itself doth the rich banquet , set before them , afford to men delight .

sections.label: Section 204
sections.original: ἰαχὰν ἄιον πολύστονον γόων ,
sections.translation: I heard a bitter cry of lamentation !

sections.label: Section 205
sections.original: λιγυρὰ δ ’ ἄχεα μογερὰ βοᾷ
sections.translation: loudly , bitterly she calls on the traitor of her marriage bed , her perfidious spouse ; by grievous wrongs oppressed she invokes Themis , bride of Zeus , witness of oaths , who brought her

sections.label: Section 210
sections.original: Ἑλλάδ ’ ἐς ἀντίπορον
sections.translation: unto Hellas , the land that fronts the strand of Asia , o ’ er the sea by night through ocean ’ s boundless gate .

sections.label: Section 214
sections.original: Κορίνθιαι γυναῖκες , ἐξῆλθον δόμων ,
sections.translation: From the house I have come forth , Corinthian ladies ,

sections.label: Section 215
sections.original: μή μοί τι μέμφησθ ’· οἶδα γὰρ πολλοὺς βροτῶν
sections.translation: for fear lest you be blaming me ; [*] for well I know that amongst men many by showing pride have gotten them an ill name and a reputation for indifference , both those who shun men ’ s gaze and those who move amid the stranger crowd , and likewise they who choose a quiet walk in life . For there is no just discernment in the eyes of men ,

sections.label: Section 220
sections.original: ὅστις πρὶν ἀνδρὸς σπλάγχνον ἐκμαθεῖν σαφῶς
sections.translation: for they , or ever they have surely learnt their neighbour ’ s heart , loathe him at first sight , though never wronged by him ; and so a stranger most of all should adopt a city ’ s views ; nor do I commend that citizen , who , in the stubbornness of his heart , from churlishness resents the city ’ s will .

sections.label: Section 225
sections.original: ἐμοὶ δ ’ ἄελπτον πρᾶγμα προσπεσὸν τόδε
sections.translation: But on me hath fallen this unforeseen disaster , and sapped my life ; ruined I am , and long to resign the boon of existence , kind friends , and die . For he who was all the world to me , as well thou knowest , hath turned out the veriest villain , my own husband .

sections.label: Section 230
sections.original: πάντων δ ’ ὅσ ’ ἔστ ’ ἔμψυχα καὶ γνώμην ἔχει
sections.translation: Of all things that have life and sense we women are the most hapless creatures ; first must we buy a husband at an exorbitant price , and o ’ er ourselves a tyrant set which is an evil worse than the first ;

sections.label: Section 235
sections.original: κἀν τῷδ ’ ἀγὼν μέγιστος , ἢ κακὸν λαβεῖν
sections.translation: and herein lies the most important issue , whether our choice be good or bad . For divorce is discreditable to women , nor can we disown our lords . Next must the wife , coming as she does to ways and customs new , since she hath not learnt the lesson in her home , have a diviner ’ s eye to see

sections.label: Section 240
sections.original: ὅτῳ μάλιστα χρήσεται ξυνευνέτῃ .
sections.translation: how best to treat the partner of her life . If haply we perform these tasks with thoroughness and tact , and the husband live with us , without resenting the yoke , our life is a happy one ; if not , ’ twere best to die . But when a man is vexed with what he finds indoors ,

sections.label: Section 245
sections.original: ἔξω μολὼν ἔπαυσε καρδίαν ἄσης ·
sections.translation: he goeth forth and rids his soul of its disgust , betaking him to some friend or comrade of like age ; whilst we must needs regard his single self .

sections.label: Section 249
sections.original: ζῶμεν κατ ’ οἴκους , οἳ δὲ μάρνανται δορί ·
sections.translation: And yet they say we live secure at home , while they are at the wars ,

sections.label: Section 250
sections.original: κακῶς φρονοῦντες · ὡς τρὶς ἂν παρ ’ ἀσπίδα
sections.translation: with their sorry reasoning , for I would gladly take my stand in battle array three times o ’ er , than once give birth .

sections.label: Section 252
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ οὐ γὰρ αὑτὸς πρὸς σὲ κἄμ ’ ἥκει λόγος ·
sections.translation: But enough ! this language suits not thee as it does me ; thou hast a city here , a father ’ s house , some joy in life , and friends to share thy thoughts ,

sections.label: Section 255
sections.original: ἐγὼ δ ’ ἔρημος ἄπολις οὖσ ’ ὑβρίζομαι
sections.translation: but I am destitute , without a city , and therefore scorned by my husband , a captive I from a foreign shore , with no mother , brother , or kinsman in whom to find a new haven of refuge from this calamity . Wherefore this one boon and only this I wish to win from thee ,—

sections.label: Section 260
sections.original: ἤν μοι πόρος τις μηχανή τ ’ ἐξευρεθῇ
sections.translation: thy silence , if haply I can some way or means devise to avenge me on my husband for this cruel treatment , and on the man who gave to him his daughter , and on her who is his wife . For though a woman be timorous enough in all else , and as regards courage , a coward at the mere sight of steel ,

sections.label: Section 265
sections.original: ὅταν δ ’ ἐς εὐνὴν ἠδικημένη κυρῇ ,
sections.translation: yet in the moment she finds her honour wronged , no heart is filled with deadlier thoughts than hers .

sections.label: Section 267
sections.original: δράσω τάδ ’· ἐνδίκως γὰρ ἐκτείσῃ πόσιν ,
sections.translation: This will I do ; for thou wilt be taking a just vengeance on thy husband , Medea . That thou shouldst mourn thy lot surprises me not . But lo ! I see Creon , king of this land coming hither ,

sections.label: Section 270
sections.original: στείχοντα , καινῶν ἄγγελον βουλευμάτων .
sections.translation: to announce some new resolve .

sections.label: Section 271
sections.original: σὲ τὴν σκυθρωπὸν καὶ πόσει θυμουμένην ,
sections.translation: Hark thee , Medea , I bid thee take those sullen looks and angry thoughts against thy husband forth from this land in exile , and with thee take both thy children and that without delay , for I am judge in this sentence ,

sections.label: Section 275
sections.original: τοῦδ ’ εἰμί , κοὐκ ἄπειμι πρὸς δόμους πάλιν ,
sections.translation: and I will not return unto my house till I banish thee beyond the borders of the land .

sections.label: Section 277
sections.original: αἰαῖ · πανώλης ἡ τάλαιν ’ ἀπόλλυμαι .
sections.translation: Ah , me ! now is utter destruction come upon me , unhappy that I am ! For my enemies are bearing down on me full sail , nor have I any landing-place to come at in my trouble .

sections.label: Section 280
sections.original: ἐρήσομαι δὲ καὶ κακῶς πάσχουσ ’ ὅμως ·
sections.translation: Yet for all my wretched plight I will ask thee , Creon , wherefore dost thou drive me from the land ?

sections.label: Section 282
sections.original: δέδοικά σ ’— οὐδὲν δεῖ παραμπίσχειν λόγους —
sections.translation: I fear thee ,— no longer need I veil my dread ’ neath words ,— lest thou devise against my child some cureless ill . Many things contribute to this fear of mine ;

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: σοφὴ πέφυκας καὶ κακῶν πολλῶν ἴδρις ,
sections.translation: thou art a witch by nature , expert in countless sorceries , and thou art chafing for the loss of thy husband ’ s affection . I hear , too , so they tell me , that thou dost threaten the father of the bride , her husband , and herself with some mischief ; wherefore I will take precautions ere our troubles come .

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: κρεῖσσον δέ μοι νῦν πρός σ ’ ἀπεχθέσθαι , γύναι ,
sections.translation: For ’ tis better for me to incur thy hatred now , lady , than to soften my heart and bitterly repent it hereafter .

sections.label: Section 292
sections.original: φεῦ φεῦ .
sections.translation: Alas ! this is not now the first time , but oft before , O Creon , hath my reputation injured me and caused sore mischief . Wherefore whoso is wise in his generation ought

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: παῖδας περισσῶς ἐκδιδάσκεσθαι σοφούς ·
sections.translation: never to have his children taught to be too clever ; for besides the reputation they get for idleness , they purchase bitter odium from the citizens . For if thou shouldst import new learning amongst dullards , thou will be thought a useless trifler , void of knowledge ;

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: τῶν δ ’ αὖ δοκούντων εἰδέναι τι ποικίλον
sections.translation: while if thy fame in the city o ’ ertops that of the pretenders to cunning knowledge , thou wilt win their dislike . I too myself share in this ill-luck . Some think me clever and hate me , others say I am too reserved , and some the very reverse ; [*]

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: τοῖς δ ’ αὖ προσάντης · εἰμὶ δ ’ οὐκ ἄγαν σοφή .
sections.translation: others find me hard to please and not so very clever after all . Be that as it may , thou dost fear me lest I bring on thee something to mar thy harmony . Fear me not , Creon , ray position scarce is such that I should seek to quarrel with princes . Why should I , for how hast thou injured me ? Thou hast betrothed thy daughter

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: ὅτῳ σε θυμὸς ἦγεν . ἀλλ ’ ἐμὸν πόσιν
sections.translation: where thy fancy prompted thee . No , ’ tis my husband I hate , though I doubt not thou hast acted wisely herein . And now I grudge not thy prosperity ; betroth thy child , good luck to thee , but let me abide in this land , for though I have been wronged

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: σιγησόμεσθα , κρεισσόνων νικώμενοι .
sections.translation: I will be still and yield to my superiors .

sections.label: Section 316
sections.original: λέγεις ἀκοῦσαι μαλθάκ ’, ἀλλ ’ ἔσω φρενῶν
sections.translation: Thy words are soft to hear , but much I dread lest thou art devising some mischief in thy heart , and less than ever do I trust thee now ; for a cunning woman , and man likewise ,

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: ῥᾴων φυλάσσειν ἢ σιωπηλὸς σοφός .
sections.translation: is easier to guard against when quick-tempered than when taciturn . Nay , begone at once ! speak me no speeches , for this is decreed , nor hast thou any art whereby thou shalt abide amongst us , since thou hatest me .

sections.label: Section 324
sections.original: μή , πρός σε γονάτων τῆς τε νεογάμου κόρης .
sections.translation: O , say not so ! by thy knees and by thy daughter newly-wed , I do implore !

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: λόγους ἀναλοῖς · οὐ γὰρ ἂν πείσαις ποτέ .
sections.translation: Thou wastest words ; thou wilt never persuade me .

sections.label: Section 326
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ ἐξελᾷς με κοὐδὲν αἰδέσῃ λιτάς ;
sections.translation: What , wilt thou banish me , and to my prayers no pity yield ?

sections.label: Section 327
sections.original: φιλῶ γὰρ οὐ σὲ μᾶλλον ἢ δόμους ἐμούς .
sections.translation: I will , for I love not thee above my own family .

sections.label: Section 328
sections.original: ὦ πατρίς , ὥς σου κάρτα νῦν μνείαν ἔχω .
sections.translation: O my country ! what fond memories I have of thee in this hour !

sections.label: Section 329
sections.original: πλὴν γὰρ τέκνων ἔμοιγε φίλτατον πολύ .
sections.translation: Yea , for I myself love my city best of all things save my children .

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: φεῦ φεῦ , βροτοῖς ἔρωτες ὡς κακὸν μέγα .
sections.translation: Ah me ! ah me ! to mortal man how dread a scourge is love !

sections.label: Section 331
sections.original: ὅπως ἄν , οἶμαι , καὶ παραστῶσιν τύχαι .
sections.translation: That , I deem , is according to the turn our fortunes take .

sections.label: Section 332
sections.original: Ζεῦ , μὴ λάθοι σε τῶνδ ’ ὃς αἴτιος κακῶν .
sections.translation: O Zeus ! let not the author of these my troubles escape thee .

sections.label: Section 333
sections.original: ἕρπ ’, ὦ ματαία , καί μ ’ ἀπάλλαξον πόνων .
sections.translation: Begone , thou silly woman , and free me from my toil .

sections.label: Section 334
sections.original: πονοῦμεν ἡμεῖς κοὐ πόνων κεχρήμεθα .
sections.translation: The toil is mine , no lack of it .

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: τάχ ’ ἐξ ὀπαδῶν χειρὸς ὠσθήσῃ βίᾳ .
sections.translation: Soon wilt thou be thrust out forcibly by the hand of servants .

sections.label: Section 336
sections.original: μὴ δῆτα τοῦτό γ ’, ἀλλά σ ’ αἰτοῦμαι , Κρέον . . .
sections.translation: Not that , not that , I do entreat thee , Creon !

sections.label: Section 337
sections.original: ὄχλον παρέξεις , ὡς ἔοικας , ὦ γύναι .
sections.translation: Thou wilt cause disturbance yet , it seems .

sections.label: Section 338
sections.original: φευξούμεθ ’· οὐ τοῦθ ’ ἱκέτευσα σοῦ τυχεῖν .
sections.translation: I will begone ; I ask thee not this boon to grant .

sections.label: Section 339
sections.original: τί δαὶ βιάζῃ κοὐκ ἀπαλλάσσῃ χερός ;
sections.translation: Why then this violence ? why dost thou not depart ?

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: μίαν με μεῖναι τήνδ ’ ἔασον ἡμέραν
sections.translation: Suffer me to abide this single day and devise some plan for the manner of my exile , and means of living for my children , since their father cares not to provide his babes therewith . Then pity them ; thou too hast children of thine own ;

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: πέφυκας · εἰκὸς δ ’ ἐστὶν εὔνοιάν σ ’ ἔχειν .
sections.translation: thou needs must have a kindly heart . For my own lot I care naught , though I an exile am , but for those babes I weep , that they should learn what sorrow means .

sections.label: Section 348
sections.original: ἥκιστα τοὐμὸν λῆμ ’ ἔφυ τυραννικόν ,
sections.translation: Mine is a nature anything but harsh ; full oft by showing pity have I suffered shipwreck ;

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: καὶ νῦν ὁρῶ μὲν ἐξαμαρτάνων , γύναι ,
sections.translation: and now albeit I clearly see my error , yet shalt thou gain this request , lady ; but I do forewarn thee , if to-morrow ’ s rising sun shall find thee and thy children within the borders of this land , thou diest ; my word is spoken and it will not lie .

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: νῦν δ ’, εἰ μένειν δεῖ , μίμν ’ ἐφ ’ ἡμέραν μίαν ·
sections.translation: So now , if abide thou must , stay this one day only , for in it thou canst not do any of the fearful deeds I dread .

sections.label: Section 357
sections.original: δύστανε γύναι ,
sections.translation: Ah ! poor lady , woe is thee ! Alas , for thy sorrows ! Whither wilt thou turn ? What protection ,

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: ἦ δόμον ἢ χθόνα σωτῆρα κακῶν
sections.translation: what home or country to save thee from thy troubles wilt thou find ? O Medea , in what a hopeless sea of misery heaven hath plunged thee !
